title: Profiteroles
subtitle: Choux buns filled with whipped cream and drizzled with hot chocolate sauce
excerpt: Profiteroles: small choux buns baked until crisp and hollow, filled with lightly sweetened whipped cream, and drizzled with a hot dark chocolate sauce. Assembled and served immediately so the contrast between cold cream and hot sauce is intact.
sourceNotes: Profiteroles as choux buns filled with cream and topped with chocolate sauce are documented in French domestic cookery from the late nineteenth century. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), describes profiteroles in the modern sense. The simplified domestic version (choux buns, whipped cream, hot chocolate sauce) is widely reproduced in twentieth-century British baking guides and is fully public-domain.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfilled buns. Defrost at room temperature and re-crisp at 160°C fan for 5 minutes before filling.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the shells the day before. Fill and serve immediately at the table.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven; reduce to 180°C after 15 minutes

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, 15 minutes at 200°C then 20 to 25 minutes at 180°C until golden and hollow-sounding

glossaryTerms.definition: Profiteroles are best served as soon as they are filled, with the chocolate sauce poured hot at the table. If you fill them in advance, the cream softens the shell and the contrast of textures is lost. The shells can be kept in an airtight container at room temperature for up to 2 days; fill and sauce immediately before serving. The chocolate sauce can be made an hour ahead and kept warm in a small saucepan over the lowest heat, or reheated gently.

content.text: Profiteroles and éclairs use the same choux pastry. The only differences are shape (rounds rather than fingers) and serving style (assembled at the table with hot sauce rather than pre-glazed). The choux method is the same in both.
Make and bake the choux buns

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Bring 200 ml water and the {{unsalted-butter}} to a rolling boil. Off the heat, beat in the sifted {{plain-flour}} until the mixture pulls cleanly from the sides of the pan. Return to medium heat and stir vigorously for 2 minutes to dry the paste. Cool for 5 minutes. Add the beaten {{eggs}} gradually, beating well between each addition, until the paste is smooth and falls from a spoon in a thick V. Pipe or spoon 24 rounds (about 3 cm) onto lined baking trays, spacing well apart. Bake for 15 minutes at 200°C fan, reduce to 180°C fan, and bake for a further 20 to 25 minutes until deep gold. Pierce the base of each bun and return to the oven for 5 more minutes. Cool on a rack.
Heat the {{double-cream}} (sauce portion) until just below a simmer. Pour over the chopped {{dark-chocolate}}, leave for 2 minutes, and stir until smooth. Keep warm.
Whip the {{double-cream}} (filling portion) with the {{icing-sugar}} to soft peaks. Split each bun and pipe or spoon in the cream. Read the
: pile the filled buns on a plate and pour the hot chocolate sauce over immediately at the table.
